The United Democratic Front (UDF) is the Indian National Congress-led alliance of political parties in the Indian state of Kerala.
File:Flag of the Indian Union Muslim League.svg
98<ref name="The Times of India-2026" /><ref name="The Economic Times-2026" />
22<ref name="@onmanorama-2026b" /><ref name="Karma News - Voice of common people-2026" />

In February 2026, the Left Democratic Front organized ''"Vikasana Munnetta Jadha"'' ("Development Advancement March"), a political march aimed at highlighting the developmental achievements of the incumbent government.<ref></ref>
* A 60-point programme containing 950 proposals to create a "''Nava Kerala''" ("New Kerala").
* Eradicating absolute poverty by uplifting 5 lakh families.
* Increase in social welfare pension from ₹2,000 to ₹3,000.
* Assured job opportunities for youth educated in Kerala through campus placements.
* "Back to Campus" scheme for skill enhancement.
* Rapid transit systems in Thiruvananthapuram and Kozhikode.
* Timely completion of the Sabarimala Railway.
* Monthly allowance of ₹1,000 for college-going girls
* Increase in social welfare pension from ₹2,000 to ₹3,000
* Interest-free loans up to ₹5 lakh for young entrepreneurs
* Dedicated department for senior citizens' welfare are protection.
* Free bus travel for women in Kerala State Road Transport Corporation buses.
* "Oommen Chandy Health Insurance" scheme providing ₹25 lakh coverage per household.
* "Bhakshya-Arogya Suraksha Card" providing monthly ₹2,500 support to women from poor and Below Poverty Line households.
* ₹3,000 monthly social welfare pension for women heads of poor households, widows, and senior citizens
* 20,000 liters of free water supply per household per month
* Two free LPG cylinders annually for poor households during Onam and Christmas
* Establishment of an All India Institutes of Medical Sciences.
* High-speed railway network connecting Thiruvananthapuram district and Kannur district districts.

In June 2022, three members–O. Panneerselvam, P. H. Manoj Pandian, and R. Vaithilingam were expelled from the AIADMK.<ref></ref> In August 2022, P. Ayyappan of the AIADMK also joined the expelled faction.<ref></ref> K. A. Sengottaiyan was expelled from AIADMK in October 2025,<ref></ref> and subsequently resigned from his position as a member of the assembly in November 2025.<ref></ref> Manoj Pandian resigned as a member of the assembly in November 2025 and joined the DMK,<ref></ref> with Vaithilingam following suit in January 2026.<ref></ref> In late 2025, the Pattali Makkal Katchi (PMK) split into two factions, with three members expressing support to Anbumani Ramadoss and two members supporting S. Ramadoss.<ref></ref><ref></ref> After Anbumani was recognised as the official leader of the PMK, Ramadoss formed a Pattali Makkal Katchi (S. Ramadoss faction) of the PMK.<ref></ref> Two members–T. K. Amulkandasami (AIADMK) and K. Ponnusamy (DMK politician) (DMK) died on 21 June 2025 and 23 October 2025 respectively.<ref></ref><ref></ref> On 27 February 2026, Pannerselvam and Ayyappan resigned from the assembly and joined the DMK.<ref></ref>

According to a report by the Association for Democratic Reforms, 404 out of the 722 contesting candidates from major parties had serious criminal cases pending them. Among the parties, 60 out of 170 candidates (35.3%) from the AIADMK, 43 out of 231 (18.6%) from the TVK, 32 out of 175 (18.3%) from the DMK, nine out of 33 (27.3%) from the BJP, five out of 28 (17.9%) from the Congress, six out of 18 (33.3%) from the PMK, one each from the DMDK and CPI declared serious criminal cases in their affidavits.<ref></ref>
Thiru. Vi. Ka. Nagar Assembly constituency (SC)
K. V. K. S. Sabari Karthikeyan
K. K. S. S. R. Ramachandran
Following an order of the Madras High Court,<ref></ref> the Government of Tamil Nadu announced new rules and guidelines for political rallies in the state in January 2026.<ref></ref>

On 7 July 2025, AIADMK general secretary Palaniswami launched a statewide campaign with the slogan "Makkalai Kaappom, Thamizhagathai Meetpom" (Let's Protect the People, Let's Save Tamil Nadu).<ref></ref><ref></ref>

alliance2 = <!-- Do not insert any placeholder for "no alliance". -->
title = Chief Minister of West Bengal

The campaign was shaped by disputes over electoral rolls and citizenship, border security and undocumented migration, and broader debates over identity, governance, women's safety, employment, development, and anti-incumbency after 15 years of AITC rule.<ref name="HinduContinuity"></ref><ref name="FederalIssues"></ref><ref name="HinduKeyIssues"></ref>

The Special Intensive Revision (SIR) of electoral rolls became one of the central controversies of the campaign. The SIR removed around 9 million voters from the rolls in West Bengal, representing about 12% of the electorate. Over six million were categorized as absentee or deceased, while the status of 2.7 million remained pending before tribunals. Observers noted that roughly 65% of the undecided group were Muslims, while Dalit Hindus, especially from the Matua Mahasangha community, were also affected in certain districts.<ref></ref>

The Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019 (CAA) remained a separate campaign issue, particularly in areas where refugee and Matua politics were significant. The Union government published details of the act's implementation in the Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019#Citizenship (Amendment) Act Rules, 2024, in March 2024, and BJP leaders said that a BJP government in West Bengal would speed up citizenship processing under the Act.<ref name="CAAOfficial"></ref><ref></ref><ref></ref> The AITC argued that the issue was being used to polarise the electorate and that the overlap between CAA, SIR and citizenship rhetoric had increased anxiety among minorities and some migrant communities.<ref></ref><ref></ref>

Border security and undocumented migration were also major issues in the campaign. BJP leaders framed cross-border movement, border fencing and the Siliguri Corridor as matters of national security and linked them to their wider argument on infiltration.<ref></ref><ref></ref> The AITC rejected these claims as politically motivated and linked them to communal polarisation.<ref name="FederalIssues" /><ref></ref>

Identity politics remained significant in the campaign. Bengali ''asmita'' (identity), the Matua vote, and questions of language, belonging and representation were prominent themes in several regions of the state.<ref name="HinduKeyIssues" /><ref name="FederalIssues" /> The AITC sought to present itself as the defender of Bengali identity and state autonomy, while the BJP tied identity questions to citizenship, migration and Hindu consolidation in selected constituencies.<ref name="HinduKeyIssues" /><ref></ref>

Corruption and governance remained important opposition themes, especially because of the 2022 West Bengal School Service Commission recruitment scam and other ongoing investigations by central agencies. These allegations formed a major opposition line of attack on the AITC government's record in administration, public recruitment and institutional credibility.<ref></ref><ref></ref><ref></ref> The AITC, by contrast, emphasised welfare delivery, continuity and its development record.<ref name=":0"></ref><ref name="HinduContinuity" />

Women's safety was a recurring issue in the campaign, especially after the 2024 Kolkata rape and murder case, which drew national attention and remained part of the wider law and order debate.<ref></ref><ref></ref><ref name="FederalIssues" /> The AITC cited National Crime Records Bureau data showing Kolkata's comparatively low reported crime rate among large cities, while the BJP argued that such data did not capture under-reporting or failures to register cases.<ref></ref><ref></ref>

Employment, industrial development and public recruitment were also prominent issues, particularly among younger and urban voters. Concerns about job creation, delayed recruitment examinations, the effects of the recruitment scandal, and competing claims over investment and industrial growth featured prominently in the campaign.<ref></ref><ref></ref><ref name=":1"></ref> The BJP foregrounded jobs and industrial revival in its criticism of the state government, while the AITC campaigned on welfare schemes and promised continued investment and infrastructure expansion.<ref name=":0" /><ref></ref>

Anti-incumbency after 15 years of AITC rule was also noted as a factor in the contest, although it overlapped with disputes over SIR, identity and citizenship.<ref name="HinduContinuity" /><ref name="HinduKeyIssues" /><ref name=":1" /> Dissatisfaction over jobs, corruption, governance and law and order was often discussed alongside these issues rather than separately.<ref name="FederalIssues" /><ref name="IESIR" />

BJP released the first list of 144 candidates on 16 March 2026;<ref name="The Economic Times-2026a"></ref> the second list of 111 candidates (also included replacement of a candidate and support to an independent candidate) on 19 March 2026.<ref name="The Economic Times-2026b"></ref><ref name="thefederal.com-2026"></ref> The third list of BJP candidates was released on 25 March 2026 wherein 19 candidates were named.<ref></ref> BJP released the fourth list of candidates on 31 March 2026 announcing 13 candidates and another 1 previously announced candidate was replaced.<ref></ref>

AITC+ released their first list of 3 candidates on 15 March 2026, including 3 candidates for Bharatiya Gorkha Prajatantrik Morcha.<ref name="The Times of India-2026b" /> Trinamool Congress released their list of 291 candidates on 17 March 2026.<ref name="The Hindu-2026"></ref>
INC released first list of 284 candidates on 29 March 2026; INC released second list of 9 candidates on 1 April 2026.<ref></ref>
AJUP released the first list of 15 candidates on 18 March,<ref></ref> and second list of 3 candidates on 23 March.<ref></ref>
AIMIM released first list of 12 candidates on 3 April.<ref></ref>
